tier_1_regimens.setting: Unresectable or metastatic tumors that have progressed following prior treatment

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.dose: 200 mg IV Day 1, repeat every 3 weeks or 400 mg IV Day 1, repeat every 6 weeks

biomarkers.requirement: Tumor must be MSI-high or mismatch repair deficient

trial_data.n_patients: 233 patients with advanced MSI-H/dMMR noncolorectal cancers
trial_data.trial_statistics: ORR 30.8%, median duration of response 47.5 months

tier_1_regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab is a systemic treatment option for adult and pediatric patients with unresectable or metastatic, MSI-H or dMMR solid tumors that have progressed following prior treatment and who have no satisfactory alternative treatment options [1]. Not for giant cell tumor of bone. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab [2].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Unresectable or metastatic tumors that have progressed following prior treatment

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.dose: 200 mg IV Day 1, repeat every 3 weeks or 400 mg IV Day 1, repeat every 6 weeks

trial_data.n_patients: Patients with advanced solid tumors
trial_data.trial_statistics: ORR 29% in tTMB-H group vs 6% in non-tTMB-H group

tier_1_regimens.notes: TMB testing is recommended for patients with unresectable or metastatic tumors who have progressed following prior treatment and who have no satisfactory alternative treatment options [3]. Not for giant cell tumor of bone. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab [2].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Unresectable or metastatic tumors that have progressed following prior treatment

trial_data.n_patients: Patients with advanced or metastatic solid TMB-H tumors
trial_data.trial_statistics: ORR 35.3% for tTMB-H in interim analysis, 22.5% for bTMB-H in final analysis

tier_1_regimens.notes: Based on data from a randomized, open-label, phase II study in patients with advanced or metastatic solid TMB-H tumors [4].

trial_data.n_patients: 33 patients with grade 1 or 2 chondrosarcoma
trial_data.trial_statistics: 6-month PFS 47%, four patients (12%) had stable disease for >1 year

tier_1_regimens.notes: Dasatinib is a small-molecule inhibitor of kinases including SRC family, BCR-ABL, c-KIT, and PDGFRs. In a phase 2 study, 6 patients had objective tumor response and four patients had stable disease for >1 year, suggesting some tumor control [5,6].

trial_data.n_patients: 42 patients with unresectable or metastatic CCS
trial_data.trial_statistics: DCR 43% at 16 weeks, median PFS 7.9 months, median OS 17.6 months

tier_1_regimens.notes: Pazopanib is an oral, multikinase inhibitor with antiangiogenic activity. A single-arm, multicenter, phase 2 study evaluated its safety and efficacy in patients with unresectable or metastatic conventional chondrosarcoma [7].

trial_data.n_patients: 21 patients with advanced chondrosarcoma
trial_data.trial_statistics: Median PFS 5.6 months, PFS at 6 months 39.5%, 52% had stable disease

tier_1_regimens.notes: Ivosidenib is a selective IDH1 inhibitor. Testing for IDH1 mutation can be performed by NGS or targeted exon sequencing [8].

trial_data.n_patients: 21 patients with advanced chondrosarcoma
trial_data.trial_statistics: Median PFS 5.6 months, PFS at 6 months 39.5%, 52% had stable disease

tier_1_regimens.notes: Ivosidenib is a selective IDH1 inhibitor. Testing for IDH1 mutation can be performed by NGS or targeted exon sequencing [8].
For dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma. Data supporting these agents are from phase 2 studies in advanced sarcomas [7,9].

trial_data.n_patients: 5 patients with chondrosarcoma (pembrolizumab), 28 patients with relapsed sarcomas (nivolumab)
trial_data.trial_statistics: 1 partial response in 5 patients with chondrosarcoma (pembrolizumab); 1 partial response in 1 patient with dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma (nivolumab)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Efficacy data supporting PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors as monotherapy in these tumors are limited. Based on data from SARC028 and a retrospective review [10,11]. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab [2]. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV nivolumab [12].

trial_data.n_patients: 38 evaluable patients with advanced bone sarcomas, 14 with chondrosarcoma (10 CCS, 4 DDCS)
trial_data.trial_statistics: Median PFS 9.5 months for CCS, 1.8 months for DDCS; Median OS 11.9 months, ORR 5%

tier_1_regimens.notes: Combination therapy evaluated in adult patients with advanced bone sarcomas. One individual with DDCS had a complete response (>4 years) [13]. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV nivolumab [12].

trial_data.n_patients: 56 patients with advanced chordoma
trial_data.trial_statistics: CBR 64%, median PFS 9 months, 70% had stable disease

tier_1_regimens.notes: Imatinib is a TKI. Phase II trial data showed a CBR of 64% and median PFS of 9 months in the intention-to-treat population [14].

trial_data.n_patients: 32 patients with unresectable, recurrent, or metastatic chordoma
trial_data.trial_statistics: 6-month PFS 54%, median PFS 6.3 months, 5-year OS 18%

tier_1_regimens.notes: Dasatinib is a small-molecule inhibitor of kinases. No substantial differences in ORR, median PFS, or 6-month PFS compared with imatinib or lapatinib treatment [5,6].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Advanced, refractory or resistant to standard therapy

trial_data.n_patients: 34 patients with chordoma
trial_data.trial_statistics: Objective response in 4 individuals at week 12, median PFS 6.1 months (95% CI, 4.6–11.5)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Based on a non-randomized, open-label, phase II basket trial in patients with rare and ultrarare sarcomas [15]. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab [2].

trial_data.n_patients: 9 patients with advanced chordoma
trial_data.trial_statistics: 44% (4/9) achieved stable disease for at least 16 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: Based on a phase II study in patients with advanced chordoma [16].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Advanced disease resistant to prior imatinib
tier_1_regimens.notes: Imatinib in combination with cisplatin has been effective in a small series of patients with advanced chordoma resistant to prior imatinib therapy [17].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Advanced disease resistant to prior imatinib
tier_1_regimens.notes: Imatinib in combination with sirolimus has been effective in a small series of patients with advanced chordoma resistant to prior imatinib therapy [18].

trial_data.n_patients: 1 patient with treatment refractory chordoma
trial_data.trial_statistics: Response to erlotinib reported

tier_1_regimens.notes: Efficacy of EGFR inhibitors such as erlotinib has been demonstrated in patients with advanced chordoma resistant to imatinib [19].

trial_data.n_patients: 18 patients with locally advanced and metastatic chordoma
trial_data.trial_statistics: Partial response in 33%, stable disease in 39% (Choi criteria); median PFS 6 months (Choi), 8 months (RECIST)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Phase II study on lapatinib in advanced EGFR-positive chordoma [20].

trial_data.n_patients: 27 patients with advanced/metastatic chordoma
trial_data.trial_statistics: Objective response 1/27 (3.7%), 9-month PFS 73.0%, 12-month OS 86.5%

tier_1_regimens.notes: Based on data from a phase II trial in patients with advanced/metastatic chordoma [21,22].

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line therapy (primary/neoadjuvant/adjuvant therapy) for localized disease

trial_data.trial_name: POG-CCG INT-0091, Children's Oncology Group study
trial_data.n_patients: 398 patients with non-metastatic Ewing sarcoma (INT-0091); 568 patients with localized Ewing sarcoma (Children's Oncology Group study)
trial_data.trial_statistics: 5-year EFS 69% vs 54% for VACD-IE vs VACD alone (INT-0091); 5-year EFS 73% vs 65% for every-2-week vs every-3-week schedule (Children's Oncology Group study)

tier_1_regimens.notes: VDC-IE is the preferred regimen for localized disease and is a category 1 recommendation. In patients <18 years, evidence supports 2-week compressed treatment [23,24]. Dactinomycin can be substituted for doxorubicin for concerns regarding cardiotoxicity [25].

trial_data.trial_statistics: 3-year EFS rates were 73% and 74%, respectively, for VACD and VAID (EICESS-92)

tier_1_regimens.notes: VAIA is another recommended regimen for Ewing sarcoma. The EICESS-92 study investigated whether cyclophosphamide has efficacy similar to ifosfamide [26,27].

trial_data.n_patients: 640 patients between ages 2 and 49 years
trial_data.trial_statistics: EFS at 3 years 61% for VIDE vs 67% for VDC-IE

tier_1_regimens.notes: VIDE is a European induction regimen. The EURO EWING 2012 trial compared VIDE induction followed by various consolidation regimens vs compressed VDC-IE induction [28].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Primary therapy for metastatic disease at initial presentation
tier_1_regimens.notes: For metastatic Ewing sarcoma, VDC is one of the preferred regimens for primary therapy at initial presentation.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Primary therapy for metastatic disease at initial presentation

trial_data.n_patients: 120 patients with metastatic disease
trial_data.trial_statistics: 5-year EFS rate was 22% for both VACD-IE and VACD groups; 5-year OS rate was 34% and 35%, respectively

tier_1_regimens.notes: VDC-IE is a preferred regimen for metastatic Ewing sarcoma at initial presentation. The INT-0091 study showed no significant difference in EFS and OS between VACD-IE and VACD regimens in patients with metastatic disease [23].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Primary therapy for metastatic disease at initial presentation

trial_data.n_patients: 640 patients between ages 2 and 49 years
trial_data.trial_statistics: EFS at 3 years 61% for VIDE vs 67% for VDC-IE

tier_1_regimens.notes: VIDE is a preferred induction regimen for metastatic Ewing sarcoma. The EURO EWING 2012 trial compared VIDE induction followed by various consolidation regimens vs compressed VDC-IE induction [28].

trial_data.n_patients: 54 patients with relapsed or refractory Ewing sarcoma
trial_data.trial_statistics: Responses in 44% of patients (35% CR, 9% PR), 26% in continuous remission after median follow-up of 23 months

tier_1_regimens.notes: Vincristine could be added to this regimen. Cyclophosphamide and topotecan have been associated with favorable response rates in relapsed or refractory bone sarcomas [29,30].

trial_data.n_patients: 20 evaluable patients with recurrent or progressive Ewing sarcoma
trial_data.trial_statistics: Overall objective response rate 63%, median time to progression 8.3 months (16.2 months for subset with recurrent disease)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Vincristine could be added to this regimen. Irinotecan and temozolomide have been associated with favorable response rates in relapsed or refractory bone sarcomas [31,32,33,34].

trial_data.trial_statistics: ORR 68%

tier_1_regimens.notes: Combination therapy with vincristine, irinotecan, and temozolomide appears active and well-tolerated in patients with relapsed or refractory Ewing sarcoma [35].

trial_data.n_patients: 39 patients with Ewing sarcoma
trial_data.trial_statistics: 6-month objective response 26%, median OS 10.2 months, median PFS 4.4 months, 42% metabolic tumor response

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cabozantinib is a VEGFR2 TKI with inhibitory activity against the MET receptor. Based on data from the CABONE trial [36].

trial_data.n_patients: 22 children and young adults with refractory bone sarcomas
trial_data.trial_statistics: Median duration of response 4.8 months, objective response rate 29%

tier_1_regimens.notes: Vincristine could be added to this regimen. Based on a retrospective study evaluating docetaxel in combination with gemcitabine in children and young adults with refractory bone sarcomas [37].

trial_data.n_patients: 73 patients treated with IFOS vs 73 with TC
trial_data.trial_statistics: Median EFS 5.7 months for IFOS vs 3.7 months for TC; median OS 16.8 months for IFOS vs 10.4 months for TC

tier_1_regimens.notes: Vincristine could be added to this regimen. The phase III rEECur trial compared high-dose ifosfamide against topotecan and cyclophosphamide [38].

trial_data.n_patients: 30 patients with metastatic Ewing sarcoma
trial_data.trial_statistics: 8-week progression-free rate 63%, median OS 53 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: Based on data from the phase II SARC024 trial in patients with metastatic Ewing sarcoma whose disease progressed following at least 1 line of therapy [39].

trial_data.n_patients: 97 patients with relapsed or refractory bone sarcomas
trial_data.trial_statistics: ORR 51%

tier_1_regimens.notes: Vincristine could be added to this regimen. Based on three Children's Cancer Group phase I/II trials [40].

trial_data.n_patients: 28 adult patients with relapsed Ewing sarcoma
trial_data.trial_statistics: ORR 14.3%, median duration of response 4.2 months

tier_1_regimens.notes: Myelosuppression was reversible and primary G-CSF prophylaxis may be considered during lurbinectedin treatment (category 2B) [41].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Resectable with unacceptable morbidity and/or unresectable axial lesions

trial_data.n_patients: 37 patients with unresectable or recurrent GCTB
trial_data.trial_statistics: Tumor response in 86% (30 of 35 evaluable patients)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Denosumab is a fully humanized monoclonal antibody against the RANK ligand. Consider consultation with dentist prior to initial therapy. May be continued until disease progression in responding disease [42,43].

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line therapy (primary/neoadjuvant/adjuvant therapy or metastatic disease)

trial_data.trial_statistics: 3-year and 5-year OS rates were 65% and 55%, respectively, in both groups

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cisplatin and doxorubicin is a category 1 recommended regimen for first-line therapy. The combination was better tolerated compared to a multi-drug regimen with no difference in survival [44].

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line therapy (primary/neoadjuvant/adjuvant therapy or metastatic disease)

trial_data.trial_statistics: 6-year EFS 63% and OS 74% for 3-drug regimen vs 64% and 70% for 4-drug regimen

tier_1_regimens.notes: MAP is a category 1 recommended regimen for first-line therapy. Preferred in patients <40 years with excellent performance status. In the event a patient receiving high-dose methotrexate experiences delayed elimination due to renal impairment, glucarpidase is strongly recommended [45,46].

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line therapy (primary/neoadjuvant/adjuvant therapy or metastatic disease)
tier_1_regimens.notes: An intensified 4-drug regimen. In the event a patient receiving high-dose methotrexate experiences delayed elimination due to renal impairment, glucarpidase is strongly recommended [47].

trial_data.trial_statistics: Response rate 48% in patients with relapsed or refractory osteosarcoma

tier_1_regimens.notes: Based on data from a phase II trial by the French Society of Pediatric Oncology [48].

trial_data.trial_statistics: Response rate 48% in patients with relapsed or refractory osteosarcoma

tier_1_regimens.notes: Based on data from a phase II trial by the French Society of Pediatric Oncology [48].

trial_data.trial_statistics: Median PFS 3.6 months for regorafenib vs 1.7 months for placebo (HR, 0.42; P=.017)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Regorafenib has demonstrated antitumor activity in progressive metastatic osteosarcoma, delaying disease progression. Category 1 recommendation based on phase II randomized data [49,50].

trial_data.n_patients: 30 patients with relapsed and unresectable high-grade osteosarcoma
trial_data.trial_statistics: PFS at 4 months 46%, median PFS 4 months, median OS 7 months, CBR 29%

tier_1_regimens.notes: Based on a phase II trial by the Italian Sarcoma Group in patients with relapsed and unresectable high-grade osteosarcoma after failure of standard multimodal therapy [51].

trial_data.trial_statistics: 6-month objective response 12%, 6-month non-progression 33%, 17% partial response, 62% stable disease as best response

tier_1_regimens.notes: Based on data from the CABONE trial in patients with advanced osteosarcoma [36].
Effective in the treatment of patients with relapsed or refractory bone sarcomas [30].
Effective in the treatment of patients with relapsed or refractory bone sarcomas [52].
Single-agent gemcitabine has shown activity in relapsed or refractory bone sarcomas [52].

trial_data.n_patients: 38 patients with unresectable or relapsed high-grade osteosarcoma

tier_1_regimens.notes: This regimen was active in the second-line setting, but toxicity required dose reductions and/or treatment interruptions in 66% of patients [53].

trial_data.trial_statistics: 19% response rate, 35% stable disease, PFS at 4 months 42%

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cyclophosphamide and etoposide combination therapy for relapsed high-risk osteosarcoma [54].

trial_data.trial_statistics: ORR 51%

tier_1_regimens.notes: Based on three Children's Cancer Group phase I/II trials [40].

trial_data.trial_statistics: Higher histologic response rate for ifosfamide/etoposide regimen vs high-dose methotrexate/doxorubicin regimen (56% vs 39%), but 5-year OS was similar

tier_1_regimens.notes: In the event a patient receiving high-dose methotrexate experiences delayed elimination due to renal impairment, glucarpidase is strongly recommended [55].
In the event a patient receiving high-dose methotrexate experiences delayed elimination due to renal impairment, glucarpidase is strongly recommended.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Relapsed or refractory disease beyond second-line therapy

trial_data.n_patients: 18 patients with recurrent or metastatic osteosarcoma
trial_data.trial_statistics: Metabolic response in 1 patient, mixed responses in 4 patients, median OS time 25 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: Radium 223 dichloride is an alpha-emitting, bone-targeting radiopharmaceutical [56].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Unresectable or metastatic tumors that have progressed following prior treatment

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.dose: 200 mg IV Day 1, repeat every 3 weeks or 400 mg IV Day 1, repeat every 6 weeks

trial_data.n_patients: Patients with advanced solid tumors
trial_data.trial_statistics: ORR 29% in tTMB-H group vs 6% in non-tTMB-H group

tier_1_regimens.notes: Recommended for patients with unresectable or metastatic TMB-H tumors who have progressed following prior treatment and who have no satisfactory alternative treatment options [3]. Not for giant cell tumor of bone. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab [2].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Unresectable or metastatic tumors that have progressed following prior treatment

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.dose: 200 mg IV Day 1, repeat every 3 weeks or 400 mg IV Day 1, repeat every 6 weeks

trial_data.n_patients: Patients with advanced solid tumors
trial_data.trial_statistics: ORR 29% in tTMB-H group vs 6% in non-tTMB-H group

tier_1_regimens.notes: TMB testing is recommended for patients with unresectable or metastatic tumors who have progressed following prior treatment and who have no satisfactory alternative treatment options [3]. Not for giant cell tumor of bone. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab [2]. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV nivolumab [12].
Sunitinib is a multikinase inhibitor. Included as a category 2A recommendation for metastatic and widespread chondrosarcoma [9].
For dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma. Data supporting these agents are from phase 2 studies in advanced sarcomas [9].
For dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV nivolumab [12].

management_principles.overview: Primary bone tumors and selected metastatic tumors should be evaluated and treated by a multidisciplinary team (MDT) with expertise in the management of these tumors [TEAM-1]. The treatment philosophy emphasizes a multimodal approach integrating surgery, radiation therapy (RT), and systemic therapy as appropriate for the specific tumor type, grade, and stage. All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with localized bone sarcomas (e.g., osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma, localized chondrosarcoma, chordoma) where the goal is complete eradication of disease.
by_intent.approach: Multimodal therapy is standard. For high-grade sarcomas like osteosarcoma and Ewing sarcoma, this involves neoadjuvant chemotherapy (where applicable), definitive local control (surgery and/or RT), and adjuvant chemotherapy. For chondrosarcoma and chordoma, surgery is the primary curative modality, with RT used as an adjunct or for unresectable disease.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with metastatic or unresectable disease where the goal is to extend survival, control symptoms, and improve quality of life.
by_intent.approach: Treatment may include systemic therapy, palliative RT (including stereotactic techniques), and/or cytoreductive surgery/ablation for symptomatic sites. Enrollment in a clinical trial is strongly encouraged.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with high-grade localized disease (osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma) or resectable disease where pre- or post-operative systemic therapy is indicated.
by_intent.approach: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy is preferred for high-grade osteosarcoma (category 1) and is standard for Ewing sarcoma. For Ewing sarcoma, at least 9 weeks of multiagent chemotherapy is recommended prior to local therapy (category 1). Adjuvant chemotherapy following local control is also standard for these tumors.

management_principles.mdt_requirements: The core MDT should include: orthopedic oncologist, bone pathologist, medical/pediatric oncologist, radiation oncologist, and musculoskeletal radiologist [TEAM-1]. Specialists critical in certain cases include: thoracic surgeon, plastic surgeon, interventional radiologist, physiatrist, vascular/general surgeon, neurosurgeon/orthopedic spine surgeon, and palliative care physician. Additional surgical subspecialties may be included as clinically indicated.
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status (e.g., ECOG, Karnofsky) is a key factor in determining tolerance to aggressive multimodal therapy (e.g., neoadjuvant chemotherapy for osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma). For example, MAP chemotherapy is preferred for osteosarcoma patients <40 years with excellent performance status [BONE-B 4 of 7]. Palliative care and best supportive care are appropriate for patients with poor performance status.

management_pathways.title: Atypical Cartilaginous Tumors (Low-Grade Intracompartmental Appendicular Tumors)

nodes.criteria: Low-grade intracompartmental appendicular tumors (atypical cartilaginous tumors)

options.label: Intralesional excision ± surgical adjuvant
options.recommendation: Wide excision optional in expendable bones [CHON-2].

options.label: Observation
options.recommendation: Acceptable option for selected patients.

adjuvant_logic.summary: No standard chemotherapy options for conventional chondrosarcoma grades 1–3. Ivosidenib may be an option for IDH1-mutant tumors [BONE-B 1 of 7].

management_pathways.title: Low-Grade Extracompartmental Appendicular Tumors, Grade I Axial Tumors, High-Grade (Grade II/III), Clear Cell, or Extracompartmental Chondrosarcoma

options.label: Wide excision
options.recommendation: Must achieve histologically negative surgical margins. May be limb-sparing or amputation [CHON-3, BONE-A].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT may be considered for positive margins or large extracompartmental tumors (category 2B). Radiation is not needed for negative margins [BONE-C 1 of 6].

options.label: Consider RT (category 2B)
options.recommendation: Consider RT with specialized techniques (e.g., proton beam) for unresectable disease or if positive margins are likely [BONE-C 1 of 6].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable for primary treatment in this setting.

options.label: Surgical excision of all sites if possible
options.recommendation: For oligometastatic disease, surgical excision of all sites if possible [CHON-4].

options.label: Consider RT for unresectable sites
options.recommendation: Consider radiation for unresectable sites or as part of palliation [CHON-4].

options.label: Consider systemic therapy (for widespread disease)
options.recommendation: Dasatinib and pazopanib are category 2A options. Ivosidenib for IDH1-mutant tumors. Consider MGPT, TMB, MMR/MSI testing [BONE-B 1 of 7, CHON-4].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable.

options.label: Wide excision
options.recommendation: With negative margins [CHON-3].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Consider Ewing sarcoma regimens (category 2B) or doxorubicin-based regimens (doxorubicin alone or doxorubicin/ifosfamide/mesna) as adjuvant/neoadjuvant therapy [BONE-B 1 of 7].

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Ewing sarcoma regimens (category 2B), doxorubicin monotherapy, or doxorubicin/ifosfamide/mesna [BONE-B 1 of 7].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable.

options.label: Wide excision
options.recommendation: With negative margins [CHON-3].

adjuvant_logic.summary: May consider treating as osteosarcoma (category 2B) with neoadjuvant/adjuvant chemotherapy. Preferred options include osteosarcoma regimens. Alternative options: pazopanib, sunitinib, pembrolizumab, nivolumab, or sunitinib + nivolumab [BONE-B 1 of 7].

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Consider osteosarcoma regimens (category 2B). Useful in certain circumstances: pazopanib or sunitinib, pembrolizumab or nivolumab, sunitinib + nivolumab [BONE-B 1 of 7].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable.

management_pathways.title: Conventional (Including Chondroid) Chordoma of Sacrococcygeal/Mobile Spine

options.label: Wide excision ± adjuvant RT
options.recommendation: Wide excision with negative margins. Consider adjuvant RT for positive margins or large extracompartmental tumors [CHOR-2, BONE-C 2 of 6].

options.label: Consider definitive RT if unresectable
options.recommendation: For tumors considered unresectable [CHOR-2].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT (preoperative, intraoperative, and/or postoperative) may be considered for positive surgical margins or large extracompartmental tumors [CHOR-2, BONE-C 2 of 6].

management_pathways.title: Conventional (Including Chondroid) Chordoma of Skull Base/Clival Region

options.label: Intralesional excision (maximal safe excision) ± adjuvant RT
options.recommendation: Intralesional excision with maximal safe tumor removal. Follow-up contrast-enhanced MRI to assess adequacy of excision. Consider adjuvant RT for positive margins or large extracompartmental tumors [CHOR-2, BONE-C 2 of 6].

options.label: Consider definitive RT if unresectable
options.recommendation: For tumors considered unresectable [CHOR-2].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT (preoperative, intraoperative, and/or postoperative) may be considered for positive surgical margins or large extracompartmental tumors. Consider re-excision if necessary [CHOR-2, BONE-C 2 of 6].

options.label: Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Soft Tissue Sarcoma
options.recommendation: Poorly differentiated and dedifferentiated chordomas are usually managed as described in the NCCN Guidelines for Soft Tissue Sarcoma [CHOR-1].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable as per this guideline.

options.label: Multiagent chemotherapy (category 1)
options.recommendation: VDC-IE (vincristine, doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide, ifosfamide, etoposide) is the preferred regimen (category 1). For at least 9 weeks prior to local therapy [EW-1, BONE-B 3 of 7].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Neoadjuvant setting. Local therapy follows after restaging.

options.label: Wide excision
options.recommendation: If resectable. Consider preoperative RT for marginally resectable lesions [EW-2, BONE-C 3 of 6].

options.label: Definitive RT
options.recommendation: For tumors not amenable to surgery [EW-2, BONE-C 3 of 6].

options.label: Amputation
options.recommendation: In selected cases [EW-2].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant chemotherapy (category 1) is recommended following local control, total treatment duration 28-49 weeks (category 1). Consider RT for positive margins, close margins, or poor histologic response [EW-2, BONE-B 3 of 7].

options.label: Consider RT and/or surgery to primary site for local control or palliation
options.recommendation: Followed by chemotherapy or best supportive care [EW-2].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable.

options.label: Multiagent chemotherapy
options.recommendation: VAIA, VDC, VDC-IE, or VIDE regimens. Longer treatment may be considered based on response [EW-3, BONE-B 3 of 7].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Neoadjuvant setting. Local therapy to primary site is still recommended.

nodes.criteria: Local control therapy to primary site (after initial chemotherapy)

options.label: Wide excision or amputation
options.recommendation: Consider preoperative RT for marginally resectable lesions [EW-3].

options.label: Definitive RT
options.recommendation: For unresectable primary tumors [EW-3, BONE-C 3 of 6].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant chemotherapy (category 1) is recommended following local control, total treatment duration 28-49 weeks (category 1). Consider RT for positive margins or close margins [EW-3, BONE-B 3 of 7].

options.label: Excision of metastases
options.recommendation: For oligometastatic disease [EW-3].

options.label: RT to metastases
options.recommendation: Stereotactic RT/SBRT can be considered, especially for oligometastases [EW-3, BONE-C 3 of 6].

options.label: Whole lung irradiation (WLI)
options.recommendation: Consider WLI for pulmonary metastases following completion of chemotherapy/metastasectomy (category 3). 15 Gy for <14 years, 18 Gy for >14 years [EW-3, BONE-C 3 of 6].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable.

options.label: Continuing chemotherapy or palliative RT
options.recommendation: Consider RT, surgery, and/or ablative therapies for symptomatic sites. Clinical trial or best supportive care [EW-3].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable.

management_pathways.title: Giant Cell Tumor of Bone (GCTB) - Localized, Resectable Disease

options.label: Excision
options.recommendation: Intralesional excision with an effective adjuvant may be adequate. Wide excision for more aggressive stage III tumors [GCTB-2].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable.

nodes.criteria: Resectable with unacceptable morbidity and/or unresectable axial lesions

options.label: Denosumab (preferred)
options.recommendation: May be continued until disease progression, in responding disease. Consider dental consultation prior to initiation [GCTB-2, BONE-B 4 of 7].

options.label: Serial embolization (preferred)
options.recommendation: For large tumors with cortical defects or joint involvement, or sacral GCTB [GCTB-2].

options.label: RT
options.recommendation: May be associated with increased risk of malignant transformation. Consider if no other options available [GCTB-2, BONE-C 4 of 6].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable.

management_pathways.title: Giant Cell Tumor of Bone (GCTB) - Metastatic Disease at Presentation
branching_basis: Resectability of primary and metastatic sites

options.label: Excision of primary tumor and metastatic sites
options.recommendation: Treat primary tumor as for localized disease. Consider intralesional excision for metastatic sites [GCTB-2].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable.

options.label: Denosumab (preferred)
options.recommendation: Consider denosumab [GCTB-2, BONE-B 4 of 7].

options.label: RT
options.recommendation: Consider RT [GCTB-2].

options.label: Observation
options.recommendation: Consider observation [GCTB-2].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable.

options.label: Wide excision
options.recommendation: If pathologic high-grade disease is discovered after wide excision, adjuvant chemotherapy is a category 1 recommendation [OSTEO-1].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Chemotherapy is not standard for low-grade tumors unless high-grade components are found.

options.label: Perioperative chemotherapy (category 1, preoperative preferred)
options.recommendation: MAP (high-dose methotrexate, doxorubicin, cisplatin) is preferred in patients <40 years with excellent performance status. Cisplatin/doxorubicin is an alternative (category 1) [OSTEO-2, BONE-B 4 of 7].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Neoadjuvant setting. Response assessment guides adjuvant therapy.

options.label: Wide excision
options.recommendation: Reassess tumor as appropriate. Good response (pathologic <10% viable tumor) -> continue preoperative regimen. Poor response (≥10% viable tumor) -> consider changing chemotherapy regimen (category 3) [OSTEO-2, BONE-C 4 of 6].

options.label: RT
options.recommendation: For unresectable disease. Postoperative RT for microscopically positive margins or gross positive margins [OSTEO-2, BONE-C 4 of 6].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant chemotherapy is recommended for all high-grade osteosarcomas. For good responders, continue the same regimen. For poor responders, changing regimen is a category 3 option. Total perioperative chemotherapy duration is typically ~30 weeks [OSTEO-2, BONE-B 4 of 7].

options.label: Chemotherapy
options.recommendation: Followed by wide excision of the primary tumor. For pulmonary metastasectomy if not possible, consider ablation [OSTEO-3, BONE-B 4 of 7].

options.label: Metastasectomy
options.recommendation: For pulmonary, visceral, or skeletal metastases [OSTEO-3].

options.label: Stereotactic RT or ablation
options.recommendation: If pulmonary metastasectomy is not possible [OSTEO-3].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable.

options.label: Chemotherapy
options.recommendation: Consider testing for TMB and MMR/MSI [OSTEO-3, BONE-B 4 of 7].

options.label: RT
options.recommendation: Consider RT [OSTEO-3, BONE-C 4 of 6].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable.

management_pathways.title: High-Grade Undifferentiated Pleomorphic Sarcoma (UPS) of Bone

options.label: Follow osteosarcoma regimens (category 2B)
options.recommendation: Treat with regimens listed for osteosarcoma [BONE-B 4 of 7].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treatment is integrated similar to osteosarcoma, with neoadjuvant/adjuvant chemotherapy.

items.text: Contrast-enhanced MRI ± CT of primary site
items.recommendation: Standard for all bone sarcomas. MRI is essential for soft tissue extent and surgical planning. CT may be used if MRI contraindicated [BONE-1, EW-1, OSTEO-1].
Initial evaluation and follow-up [BONE-1, CHON-2].
Chest CT (with or without contrast as clinically indicated) for all bone sarcomas. Low-dose, non-contrast CT is recommended for restaging [BONE-1, EW-1, OSTEO-1].

Consider for staging of Ewing sarcoma, osteosarcoma, chordoma. FDG-PET/CT is preferred for Ewing sarcoma. Bone scan may be used if FDG-PET/CT is negative or unavailable [BONE-1, EW-1, OSTEO-1, CHOR-1].

items.text: Skeletal survey, plain film, or CT (if multiple myeloma suspected)
items.recommendation: For patients ≥40 years with suspected bone metastasis [BONE-1].

items.text: MRI (with and without contrast) or CT (with contrast) of skeletal metastatic sites
items.recommendation: More detailed imaging of abnormalities identified on primary imaging for suspected metastatic disease [OSTEO-1].
Consider for chordoma and Ewing sarcoma to evaluate for additional lesions [CHOR-1, EW-1].
Prior to definitive treatment and periodically during treatment and surveillance [BONE-A, BONE-1].
To assess for hypercalcemia [BONE-1, BONE-A].
Prognostic marker for osteosarcoma and Ewing sarcoma. Include in initial workup [EW-1, OSTEO-1, BONE-A].
Prognostic marker for osteosarcoma. Include in initial workup [OSTEO-1, BONE-A].
For patients ≥40 years with suspected bone metastasis [BONE-1].
For male patients ≥40 years with suspected bone metastasis [BONE-1].
For female patients ≥40 years with suspected bone metastasis [BONE-1].
Optimally performed at the treating institution. Core needle or surgical biopsy recommended. Needle biopsy is not recommended for skull base tumors [BONE-A, BONE-1].

Essential for Ewing sarcoma (e.g., t(11;22)). Consider MGPT with validated/FDA-approved assay for targeted therapy opportunities in metastatic chondrosarcoma, recurrent chordoma, metastatic Ewing sarcoma, and metastatic osteosarcoma [EW-1, CHON-4, CHOR-3, OSTEO-3].
For chondrosarcoma to determine eligibility for ivosidenib [BONE-B 1 of 7].
For unresectable or metastatic tumors who have progressed following prior treatment and who have no satisfactory alternative treatment options. Not for giant cell tumor of bone [BONE-B 1 of 7].
Include in workup for Ewing sarcoma [EW-1].
Should be offered to individuals of child-bearing potential if cytotoxic chemotherapy will be given [EW-1, OSTEO-1, BONE-A].

Consider for patients with osteosarcoma or chondrosarcoma based on personal or family history of bone sarcomas or predisposition syndromes (e.g., Li-Fraumeni, hereditary retinoblastoma) [BONE-A, OSTEO-1].
Complete evaluation including assessment of symptoms and functional status [BONE-1, EW-1, OSTEO-1].

surgery.role: Surgery is the primary local control modality for most resectable bone sarcomas and is essential for cure. It is used neoadjuvantly, adjuvantly, or as definitive therapy depending on tumor type, response to systemic therapy, and resectability.
principles: Prior to biopsy, consultation with an orthopedic oncologist regarding appropriate prebiopsy imaging is critical [BONE-A].
Biopsy is optimally performed at a center that will provide definitive management. Placement of biopsy is critical to subsequent limb-sparing surgery [BONE-A].
Wide excision should achieve histologically negative surgical margins. Negative margins optimize local tumor control [BONE-A, BONE-C 3 of 6].

Local tumor control may be achieved by either limb-sparing excision or limb amputation. Limb-sparing excision is preferred to optimize function if reasonable functional expectations can be achieved [BONE-A].
Final pathologic evaluation should include assessment of surgical margins, size/dimensions of tumor, and response to preoperative therapy [BONE-A].
Fresh tissue may be needed for biomarker testing and tissue banking [BONE-A].

For specific tumor types: Intralesional excision may be adequate for atypical cartilaginous tumors of bone (low-grade). Wide excision is standard for high-grade chondrosarcoma, chordoma, osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma, and GCTB in select cases.

procedures.name: Wide Excision
procedures.approach: Resection of the tumor with a cuff of normal tissue to achieve negative margins. May be limb-sparing or amputation.
procedures.indications: Standard for high-grade chondrosarcoma, chordoma, osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma, and GCTB (for aggressive stage III or unresectable axial lesions).
procedures.key_complications: Wound complications, infection, neurovascular injury, functional loss, need for reconstruction.
procedures.operative_mortality: Low, but varies with patient comorbidities and extent of surgery.

procedures.name: Intralesional Excision (Curettage)
procedures.approach: Surgical removal of tumor contents from within its cavity, often with adjuvants (e.g., cryosurgery, phenol, cement).
procedures.indications: Atypical cartilaginous tumors (low-grade intracompartmental appendicular tumors). May be considered for GCTB (especially resectable with acceptable morbidity).
procedures.key_complications: Higher local recurrence rate compared to wide excision, especially for GCTB.

procedures.name: Amputation
procedures.approach: Removal of the entire limb or part of it.
procedures.indications: Selected cases of osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma, and other sarcomas where limb-sparing surgery cannot achieve negative margins or would result in a non-functional limb. Also for management of local relapse.
procedures.key_complications: Prosthetic complications, phantom limb pain, psychological impact.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Resectability is determined by the multidisciplinary team based on tumor size, location, involvement of neurovascular structures, and expected functional outcome. For example, chordomas of the skull base are often considered resectable via intralesional excision with maximal safe removal, while those of the sacrum may require wide excision. For GCTB, resectability also considers the morbidity of the procedure.
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Optimization of patient performance status, nutritional status, and management of comorbidities. Consider preoperative embolization for hypervascular tumors (e.g., GCTB of sacrum). For Ewing sarcoma and osteosarcoma, neoadjuvant chemotherapy is standard to downstage tumor. Fertility preservation should be discussed prior to chemotherapy.

radiation_therapy.role: RT is used as an adjuvant to surgery for patients with resectable tumors (especially for positive margins) or as definitive therapy in patients with tumors not amenable to surgery. Specialized techniques should be considered to allow high-dose therapy while maximizing normal tissue sparing [BONE-C 1 of 6].
principles: Patients should be strongly encouraged to have RT at the same specialized center that is providing surgical and systemic interventions [BONE-C 1 of 6].

Specialized techniques such as intensity-modulated RT (IMRT); particle beam RT with protons, carbon ions, or other heavy ions; or stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) should be considered as indicated [BONE-C 1 of 6].

RT doses listed in the algorithm are for conventional fractionated regimens (1.8–2.0 Gy). Alternative total dose and fractionation schemes are necessary for specialized techniques such as SRS and stereotactic body RT (SBRT) [BONE-C 1 of 6].

For Ewing sarcoma, RT should start by week 12 of VDC/IE chemotherapy or week 18 of VIDE and is given concurrently with chemotherapy, withholding anthracyclines during RT per the Womer Protocol [BONE-C 3 of 6].
Postoperative RT should begin within 60 days of surgery [BONE-C 3 of 6].

dose_frameworks.name: Conventional Fractionation
dose_frameworks.total_dose: Varies by histology and setting (e.g., 70 Gy for chondrosarcoma microscopically positive margins; 45-55.8 Gy for Ewing sarcoma primary tumor; 50-60 Gy for GCTB; 55-70 Gy for osteosarcoma).
dose_frameworks.fractions: Varies (e.g., 25-39 fractions for 45 Gy, 35-39 fractions for 70 Gy).
dose_frameworks.indication: Standard adjuvant or definitive RT for most bone sarcomas.

dose_frameworks.name: Stereotactic Body RT (SBRT) / Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS)
dose_frameworks.total_dose: Varies by site and intent (e.g., 24-60 Gy for oligometastases, 36-45 Gy for primary tumors in select cases).
dose_frameworks.dose_per_fraction: High dose per fraction (e.g., 6-24 Gy per fraction).
dose_frameworks.indication: Oligometastases, unresectable primary tumors in select cases (e.g., chordoma, chondrosarcoma), recurrent disease.

approaches.name: Adjuvant RT for Positive Margins
approaches.dose_fractionation: 55 Gy with 9–13 Gy boost to microscopic disease (total 64-68 Gy) for osteosarcoma. 45 Gy followed by cone-down to 55.8 Gy for Ewing sarcoma with positive margins.
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Yes, for Ewing sarcoma (withholding anthracyclines). Not standard for osteosarcoma.
approaches.indication: Microscopically positive margins after surgery for osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma, and potentially other sarcomas.

approaches.name: Definitive RT for Unresectable Disease
approaches.dose_fractionation: >70 Gy for chondrosarcoma/chordoma with specialized techniques. 45-55.8 Gy for Ewing sarcoma.
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Yes for Ewing sarcoma (concurrent with chemotherapy). Not standard for chondrosarcoma/chordoma.
approaches.indication: Unresectable primary bone sarcomas (e.g., chordoma, chondrosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma).
approaches.toxicities: Organ-specific depending on location (e.g., neurological, gastrointestinal).

approaches.name: Hemithorax Irradiation
approaches.dose_fractionation: 15–20 Gy (1.5 Gy/fraction) followed by cone-down to primary site.
approaches.indication: Chest wall primaries with extensive ipsilateral pleural involvement (Ewing sarcoma).

approaches.name: Whole Lung Irradiation (WLI)
approaches.dose_fractionation: 15 Gy (1.5 Gy/fraction) for patients <14 years; 18 Gy (1.5 Gy/fraction) for patients >14 years.
approaches.indication: Pulmonary metastases following completion of chemotherapy/metastasectomy (Ewing sarcoma, category 3).

approaches.name: RT for GCTB
approaches.indication: Unresectable, progressive, or recurrent disease that has not responded to denosumab, serial embolizations, or other treatments.
approaches.toxicities: Increased risk of malignant transformation noted in some studies.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is a critical component of treatment for osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma, mesenchymal chondrosarcoma, dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma, and metastatic/unresectable disease of various histologies. For conventional chondrosarcoma and chordoma, systemic therapy options are limited and largely targeted or immunotherapy-based. All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Category 1 evidence supports improved survival with neoadjuvant/adjuvant chemotherapy. MAP is preferred in patients <40 years with excellent performance status.
Category 1 evidence supports improved EFS and OS with VDC-IE regimen.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Biomarker testing (e.g., cytogenetics, fusion panels) essential for diagnosis and to identify translocations. If pathologic workup is negative, consider MGPT.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Chemotherapy improves outcomes in conjunction with surgery of primary and metastatic sites.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Consider MGPT, TMB, MMR/MSI testing for targeted therapy opportunities.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Multiagent chemotherapy is standard. Longer treatment may be considered based on response.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Etoposide/high-dose ifosfamide/mesna, high-dose ifosfamide/mesna, regorafenib (category 1), sorafenib.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on phase II/III trials showing activity in relapsed disease.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Cabozantinib, cyclophosphamide/topotecan, docetaxel/gemcitabine, gemcitabine, everolimus/sorafenib (category 2B), cyclophosphamide/mesna/etoposide, ICE, high-dose methotrexate/leucovorin-etoposide/ifosfamide/mesna, high-dose methotrexate/leucovorin, radiopharmaceuticals including radium-223.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on phase II studies showing activity.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Cabozantinib, docetaxel/gemcitabine, high-dose ifosfamide/mesna, regorafenib, ICE, lurbinectedin (category 2B).

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma may respond to osteosarcoma-type chemotherapy.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Ivosidenib (for IDH1-mutant), pazopanib, sunitinib, pembrolizumab, nivolumab, sunitinib + nivolumab.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: IDH1 mutation testing required for ivosidenib. Consider MGPT, TMB, MMR/MSI testing.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Ewing sarcoma regimens (category 2B), doxorubicin monotherapy, doxorubicin/ifosfamide/mesna.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Mesenchymal chondrosarcoma is more chemosensitive than conventional chondrosarcoma.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Consider MGPT for targeted therapy opportunities.
IDH1 mutation testing required for ivosidenib. Consider MGPT, TMB, MMR/MSI testing.

by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Imatinib, dasatinib, pembrolizumab, sunitinib, erlotinib, lapatinib (for EGFR-positive), sorafenib, imatinib/cisplatin, imatinib/sirolimus.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Consider MGPT for targeted therapy opportunities. EGFR testing for lapatinib.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Denosumab is FDA-approved for unresectable GCTB or where surgery is likely to result in severe morbidity.
Pembrolizumab is approved for MSI-H/dMMR solid tumors that have progressed following prior treatment.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: MSI-H or dMMR testing by validated/FDA-approved assay. Not for giant cell tumor of bone.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Pembrolizumab (preferred), ipilimumab + nivolumab (useful in certain circumstances).
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Pembrolizumab and nivolumab/ipilimumab have shown activity in TMB-H solid tumors.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: TMB-H testing by validated/FDA-approved assay. Not for giant cell tumor of bone.

key_regimens.name: MAP
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, nephrotoxicity (methotrexate, cisplatin), ototoxicity (cisplatin), cardiotoxicity (doxorubicin). In event of delayed methotrexate elimination, glucarpidase is strongly recommended.

key_regimens.name: VDC-IE
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, mucositis, neurotoxicity (vincristine), cardiotoxicity (doxorubicin), hemorrhagic cystitis (ifosfamide).

key_regimens.name: VAIA
key_regimens.setting: First-line for Ewing sarcoma (localized or metastatic)

key_regimens.name: VIDE
key_regimens.setting: First-line for Ewing sarcoma (localized or metastatic)

key_regimens.name: Regorafenib

drugs.name: Regorafenib
drugs.day: Daily for 3 weeks on, 1 week off

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hypertension, hand-foot skin reaction, diarrhea, fatigue.

key_regimens.name: Ivosidenib
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Differentiation syndrome, QT prolongation, fatigue, nausea.

key_regimens.name: Denosumab
key_regimens.setting: For unresectable/metastatic giant cell tumor of bone

drugs.name: Denosumab
drugs.day: Every 4 weeks (with loading doses on days 1, 8, and 15)

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hypocalcemia, osteonecrosis of the jaw, arthralgia, fatigue. Consultation with dentist prior to initiation is recommended.

key_regimens.name: Imatinib
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Edema, nausea, diarrhea, rash, myelosuppression, hepatotoxicity.

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab
key_regimens.setting: For MSI-H/dMMR or TMB-H bone sarcomas (not for GCTB)

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.day: Every 3 weeks or 400 mg every 6 weeks

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-mediated adverse reactions (e.g., pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies), fatigue, rash.

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab
key_regimens.setting: For TMB-H bone sarcomas (useful in certain circumstances)

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.day: Every 2 weeks or 480 mg every 4 weeks

key_regimens.name: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab
key_regimens.setting: For TMB-H bone sarcomas (useful in certain circumstances)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-mediated adverse reactions (higher risk than monotherapy), fatigue, rash, diarrhea.

treatment_response_assessment.title: Response Assessment and Surveillance
treatment_response_assessment.timing: After completion of neoadjuvant chemotherapy and before local control surgery (for osteosarcoma and Ewing sarcoma). Follow-up surveillance occurs at regular intervals post-treatment as specified by tumor type.
response_logic: For osteosarcoma: Response is defined by pathologic mapping per institutional guidelines. Good response is <10% viable tumor; poor response is ≥10% viable tumor [OSTEO-2].
For Ewing sarcoma: Restaging with imaging after primary chemotherapy to assess response and guide local therapy [EW-1].
For chondrosarcoma and chordoma: Response assessment typically by imaging (CT/MRI) to assess size and extent [CHON-3, CHOR-3].
For GCTB: Imaging (plain x-rays, contrast-enhanced CT ± MRI) to assess response to denosumab or other therapy [GCTB-2].
imaging_recommendations: For osteosarcoma and Ewing sarcoma: Restage with chest CT (non-contrast for restaging), contrast-enhanced MRI ± CT of primary site, and consider FDG-PET/CT or bone scan (use same technique as initial workup) [OSTEO-2, EW-1].

For chondrosarcoma: Surveillance with x-rays and/or cross-sectional imaging (CT or MRI) as clinically indicated. Chest imaging every 3-6 months for 5 years, then yearly for a minimum of 10 years [CHON-3].
For chordoma: Surveillance with imaging of primary site (x-ray, MRI ± CT) up to 10 years, chest imaging every 6 months for 5 years then annually [CHOR-3].
For Ewing sarcoma: Surveillance with contrast-enhanced MRI ± CT of primary site, chest imaging every 3 months for 2 years, then increasing intervals. Consider FDG-PET/CT or bone scan [EW-2].

For osteosarcoma: Surveillance every 3 months for years 1-2, then every 4 months for year 3, then every 6 months for years 4-5, then annually. Include physical exam, imaging of primary site and chest [OSTEO-4].
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Biopsy should be performed to confirm diagnosis of recurrence when imaging is suspicious [BONE-A].
For local recurrence: Wide excision if resectable. Consider RT or re-excision for positive margins [CHON-3, EW-2, OSTEO-4].
For metastatic recurrence: Systemic therapy, local therapy (surgery/RT/ablation) to symptomatic sites, or clinical trial [CHON-4, CHOR-3, EW-3, OSTEO-4].
For GCTB recurrence: Re-treat with denosumab, serial embolization, and/or RT. Risk of local recurrence is increased when denosumab is used prior to curettage [GCTB-3].
